description: Complete guide to arrays in C++: what are arrays, declaration, initialization, accessing elements, array size, passing to functions, and dynamic arrays. Many examples.

sections.title: What is an Array?
sections.definition: An array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations. It allows storing multiple values under a single variable name.
sections.analogy: Think of a parking lot with numbered spots. The lot is the array, each spot is an element, and the spot number is the index.

sections.title: Declaring 1D Arrays
sections.definition: Syntax: type arrayName[size]; size must be a constant expression (compile-time known) for static arrays.

sections.title: Accessing Array Elements
sections.definition: Use index (0-based) inside square brackets []. Access for reading or writing. Out-of-bounds access causes undefined behavior.

sections.title: Array Size and sizeof
sections.definition: sizeof(array) returns total bytes. Divide by sizeof(element) to get number of elements. Useful for loops.

sections.title: Array and Pointer Relationship
sections.definition: Array name decays to pointer to first element. arr is same as &arr[0]. Pointer arithmetic works with arrays.

sections.title: Passing Arrays to Functions
sections.definition: When passed to function, array decays to pointer. Need to pass size separately. Modifications affect original.

sections.title: Multidimensional Arrays (brief intro)
sections.definition: Arrays can have multiple dimensions. 2D arrays are arrays of arrays. Covered in detail in next module.

sections.title: Dynamic Arrays (Heap Allocation)
sections.definition: For arrays with size known at runtime, use dynamic allocation with new[] and delete[].

sections.title: Array of Objects
sections.definition: Arrays can store objects of any type. Constructor called for each element if initializer provided.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
sections.definition: Out-of-bounds, array decay confusion, forgetting to delete dynamic arrays, using sizeof on pointer.
